coredata.dc:title: Construction of cellulose-based hybrid hydrogel beads containing carbon dots and their high performance in the adsorption and detection of mercury ions in water

coredata.dc:description: Cellulose-based adsorbents have been extensively developed in heavy metal capture and wastewater treatment. However, most of the reported powder adsorbents suffer from the difficulties in recycling due to their small sizes and limitations in detecting the targets for the lack of sensitive sensor moieties in the structure. Accordingly, carbon dots (CDs) were proposed to be encapsulated in cellulosic hydrogel beads to realize the simultaneous detection and adsorption of Hg (II) in water due to their excellent fluorescence sensing performance. Besides, the molding of cellulose was beneficial to its recycling and further reduced the potential environmental risk generated by secondary pollution caused by adsorbent decomposition. In addition, the detection limit of the hydrogel beads towards Hg (II) reached as low as 8.8 × 10−8 M, which was below the mercury effluent standard declared by WHO, exhibiting excellent practicability in Hg (II) detection and water treatment. The maximum adsorption capacity of CB-50 % for Hg (II) was 290.70 mg/g. Moreover, the adsorbent materials also had preeminent stability that the hydrogel beads could maintain sensitive and selective sensing performance towards Hg (II) after 2 months of storage. Additionally, only 3.3% of the CDs leaked out after 2 weeks of immersion in water, ensuring the accuracy of Hg (II) evaluation. Notably, the adsorbent retained over 80% of its original adsorption capacity after five consecutive regeneration cycles, underscoring its robustness and potential for sustainable environmental applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cellulose-based adsorbents have been extensively developed in heavy metal capture and wastewater treatment. However, most of the reported powder adsorbents suffer from the difficulties in recycling due to their small sizes and limitations in detecting the targets for the lack of sensitive sensor moieties in the structure. Accordingly, carbon dots (CDs) were proposed to be encapsulated in cellulosic hydrogel beads to realize the simultaneous detection and adsorption of Hg (II) in water due to their excellent fluorescence sensing performance. Besides, the molding of cellulose was beneficial to its recycling and further reduced the potential environmental risk generated by secondary pollution caused by adsorbent decomposition. In addition, the detection limit of the hydrogel beads towards Hg (II) reached as low as 8.8 × 10−8 M, which was below the mercury effluent standard declared by WHO, exhibiting excellent practicability in Hg (II) detection and water treatment. The maximum adsorption capacity of CB-50 % for Hg (II) was 290.70 mg/g. Moreover, the adsorbent materials also had preeminent stability that the hydrogel beads could maintain sensitive and selective sensing performance towards Hg (II) after 2 months of storage. Additionally, only 3.3% of the CDs leaked out after 2 weeks of immersion in water, ensuring the accuracy of Hg (II) evaluation. Notably, the adsorbent retained over 80% of its original adsorption capacity after five consecutive regeneration cycles, underscoring its robustness and potential for sustainable environmental applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution caused by mercury is seriously harmful to aquatic organisms because of its toxicity and bioaccumulation by which the heavy metal species become concentrated over years and gradually stored in individuals, resulting in irreversible organ damages or diseases (Abdollahi et al., 2020; Melake et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2023). Generally, consumption of fishes and marine mammals is regarded as the dominant pathway of human exposure to high concentration level of mercury species (Meng et al., 2010). On account of the potential adverse effects of mercury species on human health, diverse restrictions have been recommended for the total mercury or methylmercury in different foodstuffs (Hsu et al., 2021). For instance, the maximum level of total mercury is restricted as 0.001–0.05 mg/kg for various foods except for salts (0.1 mg/kg); by contrast, the methylmercury level is limited to lie in 0.1–1.7 mg/kg in edible fungus and some carnivorous fishes as well as their products (declared by GB- 2762-2022 for national standards in China). In fact, the formation of toxic methylmercury in organisms is closely correlated to the release of inorganic Hg, especially for Hg (II) which is soluble in water and greatly influenced by industrial activities (Kwon et al., 2014; Li et al., 2024). Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop materials and technologies for the detection and removal of Hg (II) in aqueous solution to ensure the food safety.

Unlike the typical organic pollutants, heavy metal ions are not able to be degraded even in quite a long time but can be transported away from the contaminated waters (Zhai et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2023). Hence, adsorption technique is appropriate for the treatment of heavy metal species due to its special separation characteristics as well as easy operation and low cost (Siyal et al., 2018). Recently, the development of novel adsorbents for the efficient removal of heavy metals from aqueous solutions has garnered significant attention. A diverse array of adsorbent materials, including biopolymers, activated carbon, clays, metal oxides, and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), have been engineered to exhibit high removal rates for heavy metal contaminants. Despite these advancements, the inherent limitations in the adsorption capacities and regeneration of conventional adsorbents have restricted their application. Biomass materials (cellulose, chitosan, sodium alginate and so on) have emerged as a promising class of adsorbents, owing to their abundant availability and inherent reusability. Cellulose, in particular, stands out as an attractive substrate for modification due to its rich diversity and the presence of numerous hydroxyl groups on its surface, which can be readily modified for enhanced interaction with heavy metal ions (Channab et al., 2024; Hokkanen et al., 2016). Yun et al. grafted naphthalimide fluorophore onto cellulose and synthesized cellulose polyurethane (PU) films for the adsorption and detection of mercury ions in water (Yun et al., 2024); Kaur's group reported a method of modifying cellulose with 3,3-dithiopropionic acid and the product exhibited efficient adsorption for Hg (II) (Kaur et al., 2023). However, many of the reported powder adsorbents based on cellulose suffer from the generation of secondary pollution caused by difficulties in the adsorbent recycle due to the small size of the powders (Motora et al., 2021). Thus, the molding technique for cellulosic adsorbents is conducive to their practical application in the field of water treatment. The gelatinization of cellulose is a feasible way to convert powdered cellulose into large-sized hydrogels which is a kind of block-shaped porous material owing large surface area (Dong et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2018). Hydrogels are three-dimensional (3D), soft polymeric matrices that are formed through physical or chemical crosslinking, owning notable flexibility and pronounced swelling capacity. The 3D network configuration of hydrogels endows them with a natural propensity for capturing and retaining particles (El Idrissi et al., 2024; Rodrigues et al., 2024). Generally, hydrogel materials have good hydrophilicity and physical properties that are appropriate to be ideal candidates for the fabrication of functionalized adsorbents (Lei et al., 2022; Teow et al., 2018). Furthermore, cellulose-based hydrogels offer a unique set of advantages of robust biocompatibility, non-toxicity, safe handling, and facile degradation, which exhibits a promising application in heavy metal remediation.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of nanoparticles (2–10 nm) with rich fluorescent characteristics and can be employed as sensitive and selective sensors for various heavy metal ions (Atchudan et al., 2022; Chan et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2019). In addition, CDs have significant advantages over other chemosensors such as excellent water solubility, nontoxicity and stability against photobleaching (Atchudan et al., 2020; Yarur et al., 2019). Encouraged by this, CDs with special selectivity towards Hg (II) can be loaded on cellulosic hydrogels as effective sensing moieties to realize the integration of in situ detection and removal of Hg (II) from water. Nevertheless, the difunctional adsorbents containing CDs as nontoxic sensor for Hg (II) have been rarely reported (Perumal et al., 2022; Yan et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2023).

According to the above assumptions, a series of adsorbent materials are prepared with the proposed strategy that highly efficient and sensitive CDs are successfully encapsulated in cellulosic hydrogel beads. The hydrogel beads exhibit not only high efficiency in the removal of Hg (II) from water, but also excellent recognition performance in the detection to Hg (II). Contrary to the prevalent cellulose-derived adsorbents, which are predominantly in the form of loose powders, the hydrogels synthesized in this work exhibit a marked enhancement in recyclability. Furthermore, the strategic incorporation of CDs indicator particles endows the material with responsive sensing capabilities, thereby seamlessly integrating the functions of adsorption and detection towards Hg (II), indicating its practical utility in the treatment of mercury contamination.

Microcrystalline cellulose (MCC), diammonium cerium nitrate (99%) and glycidyl methacrylate (GMA, 97%) were purchased from Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide (98%), urea (99%), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5%), o-phenylenediamine (OPD, 98%), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (3-MPA, 98%), p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA, 99%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%) tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA) and calcium chloride (CaCl2, 97%) were obtained from Macklin (Shanghai, China). N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetone, nitric acid (GR) and methanol were supplied by Damao Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Deionized water was prepared in laboratory and all the reagents were used without further purification.

The synthetic route of the hydrogel is shown in Scheme 1 . The preparation of Cell-GMA refers to our previous work (Li et al., 2021). The experimental procedures are shown as follows, MCC (5 g) was dispersed in aqueous solution of sodium hydroxide (30 wt%, 50 mL) and stirred at room temperature for 90 min. After the reaction, the solid was washed to neutral and dried at 45 °C for 12 h to obtain alkalized cellulose. The obtained product (3 g) was suspended in an aqueous solution of diammonium cerium nitrate (1 wt%, 100 mL), then 0.4 mL of nitric acid was dropwise added and the reaction was stirred at 35 °C for 15 min. After that, 4 g of GMA was slowly added to the suspension and reacted for another 3 h. The purified Cell-GMA was obtained by soxhlet extraction for 12 h (methanol/acetone = 1:1).

Cell-GMA (2 g) was added to TEPA/DMF solution (0.23 g/mL, 40 mL) and the ammonification was performed at 80 °C for 24 h. The raw product was purified by filtration and washed with deionized water to neutral. Finally, Cell-NH2 was dried at 50 °C for 12 h and obtained as a yellow powder.

The nitrogen and sulfur co-doping carbon dots (N, S-CDs) were synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method. Firstly, OPD (1.46 g), 3-MPA (0.4 g) and PABA (0.7 g) were dissolved in 40 mL of DMF and then H2O2 (4 mL) was dripped into the solution. The mixture was ultrasonically treated for 10 min and transferred to a 100 mL of polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and heated at 180 °C for 8 h in an autoclave. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained solution was centrifuged for 15 min at 8000 r/min and the supernatant was collected for further filtration with 0.22 μm of micro-pore film. The solution was then dialyzed in a dialysis bag (500 Da) for 48 h. Finally, the synthesized N, S-CDs solution was stored in a fridge at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of cellulose-based fluorescent gel beads composites
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of Cell-NH2 and MCC (5 g) was dispersed into 100 g of NaOH/urea/H2O solution with weight ratio of 7/12/81. The mixture was stirred for 5 min using a magnetic stirrer at 450 rpm. The uniform solution was stored in a freezer at −12 °C for 12 h. Next, the frozen mixture was stirred at room temperature for 20 min at 1000 rpm to obtain a transparent solution. Finally, the solution was dripped into a CaCl2/HCl solution (5 wt% CaCl2, 5 wt% HCl) and solidified for 12 h to form cellulose gel beads. The gel beads were washed with deionized water until the scrubbing solution to neutral and then the beads were immersed in N, S-CDs solution (1 mg/mL) for 24 h. Afterwards, the cellulose-based fluorescent beads were obtained by freeze-drying technique. In addition, different proportions between MCC and Cell-NH2 were employed for the preparation of a series of gel beads composites and marked as CB, CB-25% and CB-50%, respectively. The details of the composite materials are summarized in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of amino-modified fluorescent gel beads (CB–NH2)
ce:section.ce:para: The composite material CB was also ammoniated with the same method as mentioned in part 2.2.2. Similarly, the amino-modified beads were immersed in N, S-CDs solution with a concentration of 1 mg/mL for 24 h. Then, the small beads were washed with deionized water. Finally, the cellulose beads were freeze-dried and the obtained CB-NH2 beads were kept in a desiccator.

The functional groups of the synthesized materials were characterized using FT-IR spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet 6700, the United States) and the surface morphology of the samples were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-4800, Japan). The crystalline structure of gel beads were recorded on X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8, Germany). Transmission electron microscopy images were used to analyse the size distribution of carbon dots (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20, the United States) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Scientific Nexs, the United States) were used to characterize the variation of binding energy for elements in different chemical environment. The change of fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CDs solution was measured using Lengguang Luminescence spectrophotometer (F97PRO, China). The absorbance of N, S-CDs solution before and after Hg (Ⅱ) interaction was tested by ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (T6 New Century, China). The residual concentrations of Hg (II) in the solution were determined by atomic absorption spectrometer (Agilent 200 series AA, the United States). In addition, the compressive strength of the hydrogels was measured with an electronic universal testing machine (MTS E43.104, China) with a compression speed of 1.0 mm/min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability of the N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: UV–Vis spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectrophotometer were used to evaluate the optical properties of the N, S-CDs. The effects of irradiation time, temperature and ionic strength were investigated in the stability experiments. Specifically, 2 mL of N, S-CDs solution was exposed to sunlight and relevant fluorescence intensity of the solutions were monitored at different time intervals (0.5, 1, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20, 24 h). To explore the temperature effect on N, S-CDs, the temperature of its solutions were set to 10, 25, 35, 45, 55, 65, 75, 85, 100 °C, respectively, and the fluorescence emissions at different temperatures were also monitored. For the influence of ionic strength, the fluorescence intensities of the solutions containing NaCl were detected across concentrations of 0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2 mol/L, respectively.

The optical response of the N, S-CDs was investigated by fluorimetric and colorimetric titration. Briefly, N, S-CDs solution (0.1 mg/mL) and various metal ion solutions (K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Hg2+) with a concentration of 100 mg/L were firstly prepared. 1–13 μL of Hg2+ solution was gradually added into 2 mL of N, S-CDs solution and the fluorescence emission was measured with excitation wavelength of 429 nm to determine the sensitivity of N, S-CDs towards Hg2+. Besides, other metal ion solutions (13 μL) were mixed with N, S-CDs solution as well and the selectivity of N, S-CDs for metal ions was evaluated by the measurement of relevant fluorescence intensity. For the Hg2+ colorimetric sensing performance, 1–15 μL of Hg2+ solution with concentration of 100 mg/L was added to 2 mL of N, S-CDs solution gradually and the absorbance of the mixed solution were measured.

The batch adsorption experiments were used to evaluate the adsorptive performance of Hg2+ ions on four kinds of gel beads. Specifically, certain amounts of Hg (Ⅱ) were added into the suspensions of 10 mg cellulosic beads and the mixtures were then shaken for 240 min at 298 K in pH range between 1 and 6. Besides, a series of 10 mg of gel beads were mixed with 10 mL of Hg2+ ion solution (50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400 mg/L) in plastic tubes, followed by shaking at 298 K, 308 K, 318 K for 240 min to investigate the influence of initial concentration of Hg (II). In addition, 100 mg of gel beads were added to 100 mL of Hg2+ ion solution (200 mg/L) and the mixture was shaken for 4 h. The adsorption capacities in different stages (1, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 45, 60, 90, 120, 180, 240 min) were recorded to analyse and understand the adsorption kinetics. All experiments were performed for 3 times to obtain average values. The concentrations of residual Hg2+ in the solutions were measured by Agilent 200 series AA. The adsorption capacity Q e (mg/g) were calculated according to eq. (1) (Yuan et al., 2020):
(1)

where C 0 and C e (mg/g) are the initial and equilibrium concentration of Hg (II), respectively; V (L) is the volume of solution and m (g) is the mass of gel beads.

A certain quantity of dry cellulosic beads was immersed in 50 mL deionized water for 24 h. After 24 h of swelling, the wet beads were taken out from the solution and the excessive surface water was removed before weighing. The swelling ratio was calculated as eq. (2):
(2)
Swelling
ratio =
W w
−
W d
W d
where W w is the mass of cellulosic beads after reaching swelling and W d is the weight of the dried gel beads.

Considering the flexible application conditions of the adsorbent, it is necessary to evaluate the stability of the hydrogel beads in water as well as the escape of fluorescent CDs to guarantee the effectiveness of fluorescent sensing. Specifically, a certain amount of adsorbent materials were placed in water and under vigorous stirring at 298 K. The adsorbents were taken out and dried after stirring for 24 h, 48 h and 72 h. The morphology of the gel beads was then observed and collected. Meanwhile, the escape of N, S-CDs in the adsorbents also needs to take into account. To be specific, 0.1 g of the material was immersed in 100 mL of deionized water for the release of CDs. Afterwards, 2 mL of the samples were taken out with a syringe and placed in a quartz cuvette for fluorescence measurement while the soaking time at 10 min, 20 min, 30 min, 1 h, 4 h, 8 h, 12 h, 24 h, 48 h and 72 h, respectively. In addition, the exhausted beads were regenerated in HCl solution (0.1 mol/L) for desorption of metal ions and the gel beads were washed with water for several times to be used in the next adsorption. The adsorption-desorption cycle process was repeated for five times.

Solvothermal method was used to prepare the N, S-CDs in a feasible way. As shown in the TEM image of the obtained N, S-CDs (Fig. 1 a), the carbon dots are evenly dispersed without apparent reunion and have typical spherical shapes. In addition, the average particle size of the samples is 2.26 ± 0.12 nm based on the statistical analysis of 111 carbon dots (inset picture in Fig. 1a). The XRD pattern of N, S-CDs is displayed in Fig. 1b and the broad peak at about 26° is corresponding to crystallographic planes (002) of graphene (Han et al., 2017). Besides, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image displays clear lattice fringes in the samples which also confirm the crystalline nature of the prepared CDs (Wang et al., 2022). The legible interplanar distance between lattice fringes is extracted to be about 0.23 nm, which can be assigned to be the (1120) lattice fringe of graphene (Wang et al., 2018). Moreover, Raman spectroscopy is often used to analyse the structural defects of carbon dots materials (Niu et al., 2016), in which the ratio of Raman peak intensity between D (sp3-hybridized carbon networks) and G (graphitic sp2-hybridized carbon) bands (ID/IG) is employed to evaluate the graphitization extent of the material (Zhong et al., 2023). As shown in Fig. 1c, the Raman spectrum of N, S-CDs exhibits a couple of peaks at 1386 cm−1 (D band) and 1580 cm−1 (G band), respectively, and the ID/IG ratio is 0.8 for the N, S-CDs, indicating the structure of the prepared CDs lies to be intermediate between amorphous carbon and nanocrystalline graphite. The peak observed at 1523 cm−1 in the Raman spectrum is assigned to A band of a carbon polycrystalline structure, which arises from the vibration of sp3 hybridized amorphous carbon atoms interspersed with disordered carbon regions. This is a distinctive spectral feature typically associated with the vibrational modes of aliphatic chains or the aromatic ring structures (Stepanidenko et al., 2020). FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1d) is collected to identify the functional groups in N, S-CDs. The broad absorption peak at 3286-3340 cm−1 is attributed to the overlapping stretching vibration of –OH group (Liu et al., 2022a). The peak at 3121 cm−1 is related to N–H stretching vibration and the absorption peak at 2520 cm−1 indicates the appearance of –SH group. The presence of stretching vibration peak of 1663 cm−1 suggests the existence of CO group (Wang et al., 2015)and the peak at about 1604 cm−1 corresponds to the CC stretching vibration. In addition, the peaks located at 1390 and 1180 cm−1 are ascribed to C–N and C–S bonds, respectively (Singaravelu et al., 2021). The abundant functional groups in the prepared CDs would bring the material sensitive fluorescence performance with exciting light. Besides, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is used to further analyse the surface chemical environment of N, S-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1e, the full XPS survey clearly demonstrates four peaks that represent C1s (284.71 eV), N1s (398.30 eV), O1s (532.60 eV) and S2p (164.11 eV). In details, the C1s binding energy (BE) band can be divided into four peaks at 284.60 eV, 285.61 eV, 286.62 eV and 288.20 eV corresponding to CC/C–C/C–H, C–N/C–O, C–S and CO, respectively (Fig. 1f) (Chao et al., 2018; Tang et al., 2018). Additionally, the amidation reaction between –NH2 and –COOH groups could be promoted under high temperature conditions since the N, S-CDs is synthesized by hydrothermal reaction which also makes it possible for the conversion of amines into pyrrole nitrogen and pyridine nitrogen (Li et al., 2019). Thus, the high-resolution spectrum for N1s (Fig. 1g) can be divided into three peaks at approximately 398.60, 399.22 and 400.31 eV that are attributed to the pyridinic N/C–N(H)–C, amino N and pyrrolic N, respectively (Wang et al., 2022). The high-resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 1h) contains two BE peaks at 531.60 eV and 532.71 eV which correspond to the CO and C–O, respectively (Ding et al., 2015). As for S2p (Fig. 1i), the BE peaks at 163.51 eV and 164.70 eV can be allotted to C–S–H–S2p3/2 and C–S–H–S2p1/2, respectively (Kou et al., 2020). The results of XPS spectroscopy are consistent with FT-IR information, which also provides evidence for determining the possible structures in N, S-CDs.

Fig. 2 a shows the UV–visible absorption spectrum and fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of the N, S-CDs. The absorption peak at 429 nm with a shoulder peak located at 385 nm might be attributed to n-π* transitions in amino or carbonyl groups in conjugated polyene or polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon structures (Yang et al., 2019). In addition, a fluorescence emission at 571 nm is observed with an excitation wavelength of 420 nm. The inset picture illustrates the N, S-CDs solution under sunlight and 365 nm UV irradiation and the significant fluorescence emission affords it potential in metal ion detection. To further understand the fluorescence emission trends, the CDs are excited with different excitation wavelengths from 330 to 500 nm and the emission spectra exhibit significant red shifts accordingly from 560 to 581 nm with a Stokes shift of 21 nm as shown in the normalization model (Fig. 2b), indicating excitation wavelength-dependent characteristic for the N, S-CDs (Zhong et al., 2023). In this case, the prepared N, S-CDs can be adaptive in a quite wide range of excitation system. The influence of temperature, illumination period (Fig. 2c) and ionic strength (Fig. 2d) on the fluorescence emission of N, S-CDs are investigated to evaluate the optical stability under rigorous environments. It can be found that the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs shows about 11% loss after 24 h of intense daylight irradiation. Besides, temperature exhibits little interference on the fluorescence in the range of 25–100 °C, demonstrating the N, S-CDs can be stable under conditions of practical application. Furthermore, the influence of ionic strength is determined by the addition of NaCl that the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs does not change significantly while the concentration of NaCl varies from 0.0 to 2.0 mol/L (Fig. 2d) and thus suggests good salt resistance for the samples. All the results confirm that the samples have good photothermal stability and the N, S-CDs possess a long life cycle during which there is no self-quenching behaviour.

The dimensions of the prepared fluorescent beads are evaluated by the measurement of 50 particles for each of the four kinds of samples with a tool ruler and the average diameter is mostly between 3 and 4 mm (Fig. 3 a). Additionally, the average density of the beads are shown in Table S2. It is found that CB, CB-25% and CB-50% have the similar small density (0.019–0.023 g/cm3) while CB-NH2 has a larger one (0.046 g/cm3) due to the post-synthetic modification. FT-IR spectra of samples in every stage during modification were collected for the investigation of structure variation (Fig. 3b and c). Compared to the pristine CB, the new peaks at 812 cm−1 and 1720 cm−1 are attributed to epoxy group and CO stretching vibration, respectively (Li et al., 2021). The obvious enhancement of the peak at 1260 cm−1 is related to the increasing amounts of C–O–C groups after GMA grafting, indicating the successful attachment of GMA on CB. For CB-NH2, the peak at 812 cm−1 is significantly weakened due to the participation of epoxy group in the reaction. Additionally, new peaks appeared at 3455 cm−1 and 1570 cm−1 are corresponding to the stretching vibration of –NH2 and bending vibration of –NH–, implying that amino groups have been successfully grafted on the scaffolds. Similarly, the –NH2 peak at 3415 cm−1 can also be observed on the spectra of Cell-NH2, CB-25%, CB-50% (Fig. 3c) (Liu et al., 2022b). Moreover, SEM images are obtained to observe the surface topography of the materials that are illustrated in Fig. 3d. In details, the surface of CB is relatively smooth while many grooves are observed on the surface of CB-25%, CB-50% and CB-NH2, which might be related to the introduction of a large number of hydrophilic amino groups. In addition, the roughness seems to be positively correlated with the proportion of amino-doping ratio that there are more grooves on the surface of CB-50% than that of CB-25%. As a contrast, CB-NH2 shows a rough and porous surface which is quite different with the one-step shaping ones. Furthermore, elemental mapping of CB-50% (Fig. 3e) reveals the sample is composed of C, O, N and S. This result not only proves the successful amino attachment, but also confirms the immobilization of N, S-CDs onto the N-containing cellulose beads. Swelling ratio is an important parameter for liquid mass transfer and the values for each sample are shown in Fig. 3f. Among them, the swelling ratio of CB-50% reaches as high as 820% which is almost twice as high as that of pristine CB (412%). Undoubtedly, the amino-doping content greatly affects the swelling ratio of samples and CB-50% has the strongest affinity for water absorption (Teow et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg (II) by N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence sensing ability of N, S-CDs is investigated by the titration experiment. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs gradually decreases with the increasing of Hg (II) ions concentration from 0 to 3.2 μM and the inset picture clearly demonstrates the Hg (II)-induced fluorescence quenching that the fluorescence intensity exhibits an 8 times of decrement. In addition, linear fitting curves for fluorescence variation ratio (I 0/I) against Hg (II) concentration in different stages (0.24–1.24 μΜ, 1.48–2.73 μM) are illustrated in Fig. S1. It can be concluded that both the two stages have excellent linear relationship with correlation coefficient R 2 = 0.9945 and 0.9912, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) of N, S-CDs towards Hg (II) is calculated by formula 3 σ/K, where σ is the standard deviation of 10 parallel blank samples, K is the slope of the calibration curve (Yuan et al., 2021), and the LOD is calculated to reach as low as 8.8 × 10−8 M, which is below the mercury effluent standard declared by WHO. Moreover, the selectivity of N, S-CDs towards various metal ions in water is evaluated by fluorescence titration. From Fig. 4b, it can be found that only Hg (II) generates the significant fluorescence quenching and other metal ions can hardly affect the fluorescence emission, suggesting quite good selectivity of the prepared N, S-CDs in mercury detection. Furthermore, the N, S-CDs also exhibits “naked eye recognition” with Hg (II) in water which is shown in Fig. 4c. There is an obvious gradual red shift of its UV–vis absorption spectrum upon the addition of Hg (II) over concentration of 0–3.5 μM and the colour of the solution changes from light yellow to reddish brown due to the emergence of absorption peak at 450 nm. Moreover, it is worth noting that an isoabsorptive point appears at 425 nm during this process which indicates the formation of a new composite owing definite chemical composition (Hu et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg (II) by hydrogel beads
ce:section.ce:para: The relative variation ratio of fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs upon addition of different metal ions is given in Fig. 4d which presents a sharp contrast between Hg (II) and other competitive metal ions and the third inset picture displays the unique Hg (II)-induced quenching effect of N, S-CDs solutions under UV light irradiation as well. Encouraged by the selective and sensitive optical response towards Hg (II), the prepared N, S-CDs are appropriate for the modification of functionalized adsorbents as efficient dopants. Additionally, the first and second inset pictures in Fig. 4d are photographs of hydrogel beads treated with different metal ions in daylight and under 365 nm UV light irradiation, which performs the same sensing behaviour as N, S-CDs. This phenomenon is caused by the successful introduction of optically active N, S-CDs composites that appends to the cellulose beads based on hydrogen bonding and the sensing ability of N, S-CDs is not affected by the encapsulation with cellulose. Specifically, the fluorescence variation of the hydrogel beads also exhibits an obvious quenching effect upon Hg (II) addition over the range of 0–15 μM (inset of Fig. 4e). Furthermore, the fluorescence variation ratio (I 0/I) against Hg (II) concentration (0.5–3 μM) is collected and the LOD is determined to be 8.8 × 10−8 M (Fig. 4f).

Besides, time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) experiment is performed on N, S-CDs before and after Hg (II) addition to investigate the sensing mechanism of carbon dots towards Hg (II). As shown in Fig. 4e, the fluorescence lifetime is insusceptible of mercury addition, indicating the fluorescence response of N, S-CDs is attributed to static quenching (Xu et al., 2015, 2019), which is consistent with the UV–vis absorption measurement. In addition, FT-IR spectroscopy of N, S-CDs and CDs-Hg (II) adduct are analyzed to further definite the interaction between Hg (II) and N, S-CDs (Fig. 4g). Clearly, the characteristic peaks of –SH, –NH2 and C–N in the sample are significantly weakened during the recognition of Hg (II), suggesting the participation of the functional groups containing N and S atoms on the surface of N, S-CDs (Zhang et al., 2022). The coordination effect and the formation of non-fluorescent complexes at ground state construct the static quenching in the mercury detection. As a result, the excellent fluorescence sensing ability and “naked eye recognition” performance of the prepared hydrogel beads towards Hg (II) give it great application potential in the mercury detection in water.

Moreover, the stability of the hydrogel beads is evaluated by the measurement of fluorescence response of the samples stored for 2 months to Hg (Ⅱ) with the pristine ones as a reference (Fig. 4h). It is found that the fluorescence emission of hydrogel beads can be well maintained after a long period of storage and all the four kinds of adsorbents still exhibit sensitive response to Hg (II). Besides, the leakage of CDs from the hydrogel beads should also be considered for its accuracy of fluorescence sensing. Take CB-50% as an example in Fig. 4i, the hydrogel beads are immersed in water for 2 weeks and the eluent samples at different time intervals are collected for the measurement. It is observed that only 3.3% of the CDs escape from the hydrogel beads after 2 weeks of soaking in water, indicating excellent stability of the immobilization of N, S-CDs on the cellulosic beads. In addition, the fluorescence sensing performance of the hydrogel beads towards Hg (II) at different pH is also investigated to evaluate the suitability of the material. The results reveal that upon a 48-h exposure to the specified aqueous matrix, the material's fluorescence intensity and morphological integrity remain largely unaltered (Fig. S2a). Notably, the material retains its acute sensitivity to Hg (II), verifying its potential for effective mercury detection and removal. Furthermore, to assess the material's robustness and adsorption efficacy in saline conditions, additional experiments were conducted in a saline medium. Fig. S2b indicates that the adsorption capacity of the material towards Hg (II) in a saline environment exhibits only a marginal decrement. This observation corroborates the material's high stability and selectivity for Hg (II) uptake.

Batch adsorptions are conducted to investigate the influence of various parameters including pH, temperature, contact time and initial concentration of Hg (II) on the adsorption by CB, CB-25%, CB-50% and CB-NH2.

The acidity is one of the important factors affecting the adsorption of Hg (II) due to its impact on the existing conformation of functional groups as well as the form of Hg (Ⅱ) ions in solution (Wang et al., 2023). It can be found that the adsorption of Hg (II) is pH-dependent for each cellulosic hydrogel beads where the adsorption capacity exhibits an upward trend with the increase of pH and reaches the maximum at pH = 5 (Fig. 5 a). In general, lower pH often leads to protonation to the amino groups in which N atoms are positively charged and the interaction with Hg (II) would be weaken because of the electrostatic repulsion. However, when the pH rises to 6, Hg (II) in the solution will precipitate in the form of Hg(OH)+ or Hg(OH)2 which also generates interference on the adsorption. Thus, the pH of Hg (II) solution is selected to be 5 as the optimal condition in the subsequent adsorption studies (Ge and Hua, 2016).

The adsorption isotherm describes the relationship between adsorbents and Hg (II) in the aqueous solution, and the adsorptive performance of the prepared CB, CB-25%, CB-50% and CB-NH2 at different initial concentrations and temperatures (298 K, 308 K, 318 K) are evaluated by adsorption isotherms (Fig. 5b–e). It can be found that higher temperature is benefit for the adsorption for all the four kinds of hydrogel beads with the increasing of Hg (II) concentration. Based on the adsorptive data, both of Langmuir (insets of Fig. 5b–e) and Freundlich isotherm models (Fig. S3) are used to further understand the adsorption of Hg (II) on the hydrogel beads. The linear forms of the two isotherm models can be represented as eqs. (3) and (4) (Song et al., 2017):

where C e (mg/L) is the concentration of residual Hg (II) ions in solution at equilibrium, Q e (mg/g) is the adsorption capacity at equilibrium, Q m (mg/g) is the maximum adsorption capacity, b (L/mg) is the Langmuir adsorption constant, and n and K F are constants in Freundlich model. The dimensionless constant R L is usually used to further analyse the affinity of adsorbents during the adsorption for Langmuir model and can be obtained by eq. (5). Generally, Langmuir model assumes a homogeneous adsorption on limited adsorptive sites located at monomolecular layer (Zhou et al., 2023) while Freundlich model takes the hypothesis that the adsorption equilibrium occurs on multilayer surface. The parameters in the two models are calculated and listed in Table S3. Compared with Freundlich model (Fig. S3), the uptake of Hg (II) at 298–318 K for various adsorbents fits much better with Langmuir adsorption that the correlation coefficients R 2 for the 12 cases all reach above 0.99 (Table S3), suggesting the dominance of homogeneous monolayer adsorption in these processes. Furthermore, the parameter values R L of the four materials all lie between 0 and 1 (0.03 for CB, 0.04 for CB-25%, 0.03 for CB-50%, and 0.04 for CB-NH2 at 298 K), indicating a favourable capture of Hg (II) by the adsorbents (Tang et al., 2011). The maximum adsorption capacity of CB-50% reaches as high as 290.70 mg/g due to its more CB-NH2-doping amounts in the hydrogel beads than the others. The Q m of CB-NH2 is calculated to be 232.56 mg/g which is smaller that might suffer from the unsatisfied efficiency by post-synthetic modification in heterogeneous reaction. Fig. 5f illustrates the diagram of adsorptive behaviour of adsorbents. Table S7 lists a comparison of a series of adsorbents used for Hg (II) detection and removal. As a result, in addition to the preeminent fluorescence sensing ability towards Hg (II) in water, the hydrogel beads also exhibit excellent adsorptive properties in the Hg (II) removal from aqueous solutions.

The influence of contact time on adsorptive performance for the hydrogel beads is investigated by the adsorption kinetics with initial Hg (II) concentration of 200 mg/L over 0–240 min (Fig. 6 a). It can be found that the removal efficiency of Hg (II) for all the four materials is quite rapid in the beginning 25 min and the adsorption equilibrium can be reached in 60 min. Pseudo-first-order (Fig. S4), pseudo-second-order (Fig. 6b) and intraparticle diffusion models (Fig. 6c) are used to analyse the adsorption behaviour. The kinetic models can be expressed as eqs. (6)–(8) (Kampalanonwat and Supaphol, 2010):

where Q t (mg/g) and Q e (mg/g) represent the amounts of Hg (II) adsorbed on the beads at time t and equilibrium, respectively. k 1 (min−1) and k 2 (g·mg−1·min−1) are the rate constants of the corresponding models, respectively. In addition, k di (mg/g·min1/2) is the rate constant at different stages of diffusion and C i (mg/g) is the intercept which correlates to the thickness of the boundary layer. The parameters of relevant kinetic models are described in Table S4. Obviously, pseudo-second-order kinetic model can better describe the Hg (II) adsorption on the hydrogels with correlation coefficients of R 2 = 0.9995, 0.9996, 0.9997, and 0.9992 for CB, CB-25%, CB-50%, and CB-NH2, respectively. The satisfied correlation between Q e,exp and Q e,cal suggests good suitability of the second order rate equation which indicates the uptake of Hg (II) is dominated by chemical adsorption by the hydrogels (Monier et al., 2010). Besides, the intraparticle diffusion model is employed to further understand the adsorption process and the corresponding parameters are illustrated in Table S5. The linear fitting curves for each sample clearly demonstrate three steps with different diffusion rates in the adsorption. Take CB-50% for instance, the presence of a large number of active sites in the first stage makes the adsorptive rate on the surface much larger (21.11 mg/g·min1/2) than the latter two stages (4.27 mg/g·min1/2and 0.30 mg/g·min1/2). With the continuous occupation of surface active sites, the adsorption rate decreases until equilibrium is reached. Additionally, the non-zero C i values for the hydrogels indicate that internal diffusion is not the only factor affecting the transfer and removal of mercury species in aqueous solution (Yang et al., 2021).

The thermodynamic parameters for Hg (II) adsorption on four kinds of hydrogel materials are calculated by Van't Hoff equation and given in Table S6. It can be observed that the Gibbs free energy (ΔG, kJ·mol−1) of the four materials is negative over 298–318 K, indicating that the adsorption process is spontaneous. In addition, the positive enthalpy change (ΔH, kJ·mol−1) proves that the adsorption is endothermic and higher temperature is beneficial to the mercury removal which is consistent with the adsorption isotherms. Moreover, the positive entropy change (ΔS, J·mol−1·K−1) indicates the increasing randomness at the solid-liquid interface (Chen et al., 2015).

CB-50% is utilized as a representative hydrogel adsorbent to investigate its selectivity towards mercury ions in the presence of various competing ions within a multivariate system, specifically K+-Hg, Na+-Hg, Mg2+-Hg, Ca2+-Hg, tap water-Hg (with relevant concentration of 100 and 200 mg/L for competing metal ions), and normal saline solution-Hg. The results demonstrate that the adsorbent exhibits a high selectivity for Hg (II) ions even in solutions containing competing ions (Fig. S5). Compared with the coordination effect with Hg (II), this noninterference with the competing ions is attributed to the weak interactions between the adsorbent and interfering ions, which are primarily physical and electrostatic in nature (Mudasir et al., 2020).

The adsorptive performance of adsorbents is closely related to the structure of the materials. Specifically, CB-50% exhibits the best removal efficiency in mercury treatment from water among the four hydrogels. As mentioned above, the amination ratio is possibly responsible for the higher adsorption capacity for CB-50% compared with its analogues. By the abundant hydrogen bonds, aminated and pristine cellulose are tightly combined and strong coordination effect towards Hg (II) is thus fabricated as shown in the inset of Fig. 6d. XPS survey is further used to analyse the adsorption mechanism of Hg (II) due to its evaluation of chemical environment for different elements. Herein, take CB-50% for instance, apart from the C1s, N1s and O1s binding energy (BE) peaks in the wide scan of XPS spectrum, a new binding energy peak can be clearly observed at 100.78 eV after Hg (II) adsorption, which is attributed to Hg4f, indicating the successful capture by the functionalized hydrogel. In addition, the BE peak of N1s (Fig. 6e) can be divided into two peaks located at 401.40 and 399.01 eV which are assigned to C–N(H)–C and –NH2, respectively. After Hg (II) adsorption, both of the two shift to higher region at 401.52 and 399.21 eV. Similarly, after Hg (II) adsorption, the peaks of O*-CO, C–OH/C–O–C and O–CO* belonging to O1s migrate from 533.12 eV, 532.69 eV and 532.72 eV–533.32 eV, 532.72 eV and 532.31 eV, respectively (Fig. 6f). The increase of binding energy is attributed to the formation of coordination bonds between N/O atoms in CB-50% and Hg (II), leading to the decrement of electron cloud density of N/O (Zheng et al., 2021). For C1s, it can be decomposed to four BE peaks positioned at 288.19, 287.51, 286.50 and 284.71 eV that correspond to O–CO, C–O–C, C–N and C–C, respectively. In contrast, the binding energy for C1s exhibits a decrease to 287.62, 287.02, 286.11 and 284.49 eV after adsorption of Hg (II) (Fig. 6g). This phenomenon is interpreted as the non-participation of C atoms in the coordination with Hg (II) while the atoms connected with C atoms might take part in the interaction with Hg (II), resulting in the increase of electron cloud density of C atoms (Li et al., 2023). In summary, the excellent adsorptive properties of the prepared hydrogels benefit from the amination modification, and the special porous structure of the beads also contributes a lot to the removal of Hg (II).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The reusability and stability of the adsorbents
ce:section.ce:para: The reusability of adsorbent materials determines the costs in practical application. The regeneration of the exhausted hydrogel beads is treated with HCl solution (0.1 mol/L) for desorption of Hg (II). As shown in Fig. 6h, the adsorptive behaviour for each kind of hydrogel exhibits a slight weakness after five cycles of regeneration that more than 80% of the adsorption capacity can be retained, indicating excellent reusability of the adsorbent materials. Table S8 lists the cost for the preparation of the hydrogel materials ($1.81/g). Concurrently, the fluorescence characteristics of the hydrogel adsorbents throughout the regeneration were assessed (Fig. S6). The result indicates that the adsorbents exhibit remarkable stability with its fluorescence emission being preserved post-regeneration. Notably, the material retains a pronounced sensitivity, exhibiting a significant quenching response upon interaction with mercury ions. These findings provide the potential of the hydrogel adsorbents for repeated use in mercury detection and removal without compromising its efficacy. In addition, the construction stability of the porous beads is evaluated with the impregnation method in which the hydrogels are soaked in water under vigorous stirring for 72 h. It is observed that the appearance of the adsorbents has no significant change during the immersion except a little expansion of the volume caused by the swelling effect (Fig. 6i). Combined with the little influence of storage period and CDs leakage on the performance of adsorption and detection to Hg (II), the gel beads are proved to be quite stable in the practical application. Moreover, the compressive strength for the cellulosic hydrogels was measured by an electronic universal testing machine. Table S9 presents a comparative analysis of the compressive strengths for a series of adsorbents. The compressive strength of CB-50% is measured at 0.075 MPa (Fig. S7), representing a mild strength in mechanical performance compared to cellulose-based hydrogels previously documented in the literatures. Even so, it is anticipated to contribute to a marked reduction in material degradation and loss during practical applications, thereby enhancing the durability and longevity of the hydrogels.

In this work, we have successfully encapsulated carbon dots with high selectivity and sensitivity towards mercury ions within cellulose-based hydrogel beads. The encapsulation process did not compromise the selective sensing properties of the carbon dots, demonstrating the versatility of this approach for the packaging of carbon dots with targeted responses to various metal ions. Moreover, the cellulose beads exhibited remarkable stability, with their fluorescence emission remaining largely unaffected by exposure to sunlight. Notably, the hydrogel beads retained their sensitivity to mercury ions even after a storage period of two months, underscoring the reliability and stability of the encapsulation technique. The robustness and longevity of the hydrogel beads in maintaining their functional integrity offer a solid foundation for the practical application of these materials in aquatic environments. The low-cost production process, which takes into account the readily available sources of cellulose and carbon dots, further enhances the potential for widespread application in water treatment. In particular, the implications for heavy metal removal and detection are profound, as these materials provide a cost-effective and efficient solution for addressing the pervasive issue of heavy metal contamination in water bodies.

Ming Li: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Panpan Zhang: Data curation, Conceptualization. Jianwei Mao: Validation. Jianfeng Li: Visualization. Yuling Zhang: Project administration. Bo Xu: Resources. Jin Zhou: Investigation. Qianyong Cao: Supervision. Huining Xiao: Writing – review & editing.
